concepts.title: Derivative of Implicit Function
concepts.conceptBody:
Differentiation of implicit function

If a relation between $\text{x}$ and $\text{y}$ is such that $\text{y}$ cannot be expressed in terms of $\text{x}$ then $\text{y}$ is called an implicit function of $\text{x}$. Here we will give method to find $\frac{dy}{dx}$ if $y$ is an implicit function of $x$Working Rule:

(i) Differentiate the given relation between $\text{x}$ and $\text{y}$ w.r.t. $\text{x}$.

(ii) Bring all the terms containing $\frac{\text{dy}}{\text{dx}}$ on left hand side and remaining terms on right hand side and then find $\frac{dy}{dx}$.

(iii) Use the given relation between $\text{x}$ and $\text{y}$ to get the result in simplified form.

 

Q) If $xy={{x}^{3}}+{{y}^{3}}$, find $\frac{dy}{dx}$. 

Solution: 

Given $xy={{x}^{3}}+{{y}^{3}}$s

differentiating w.r.t. $\text{x}$, we get (i)

$\frac{d}{d x}(x y)=\frac{d}{d x}\left(x^3\right)+\frac{d}{d x}\left(y^3\right)$

$1 \cdot y+x \cdot \frac{d y}{d x}=3 x^2+3 y^2 \frac{d y}{d x}$

or $\quad\left(x-3 y^2\right) \frac{d y}{d x}=3 x^2-y$

$\therefore \quad \frac{\mathrm{dy}}{\mathrm{dx}}=\frac{3 \mathrm{x}^2-2}{\mathrm{x}-3 \mathrm{y}^2}$

 

Q) If $x+y=\text{sin}\left( xy \right)$, find $\frac{dy}{dx}$. 

Solution: 

Given $\text{x}+\text{y}=\text{sin}\left( \text{xy} \right)$

differentiating w.r.t. $\text{x}$, we get (ii)

$1+\frac{dy}{dx}=\frac{d}{dx}\left( \text{sin}\left( xy \right) \right)=\frac{d}{dx}\text{sin}\left( xy \right)\cdot \frac{d}{dx}\left( xy \right)$

$=\cos (x y) \cdot\left(1 \cdot y+x \frac{d y}{d x}\right)=y \cos (x y)+x \cos (x y) \frac{d y}{d x}$

$\therefore \quad[1-x \cos (x y)] \frac{d y}{d x}=y \cos (x y)-1$

$\therefore \quad \frac{\mathrm{dy}}{\mathrm{dx}}=\frac{\mathrm{y} \cos (\mathrm{xy})-1}{1-\mathrm{x} \cos (\mathrm{xy})}$

 

Q) If ${{x}^{3}}+{{y}^{3}}=\text{sin}\left( x+y \right)$, find $\frac{dy}{dx}$. 

Solution: 

Given, ${{x}^{3}}+{{y}^{3}}=\text{sin}\left( x+y \right)$

differentiating w.r.t. $\text{x}$, we get

$\frac{\mathrm{d}}{\mathrm{dx}}\left(\mathrm{x}^3\right)+\frac{\mathrm{d}}{\mathrm{dx}}\left(\mathrm{y}^3\right)=\frac{\mathrm{d}}{\mathrm{dx}}[\sin (\mathrm{x}+\mathrm{y})]$

$\frac{d}{d x}\left(x^3\right)+\frac{d}{d x}\left(y^3\right) \frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{d \sin (x+y)}{d(x+y)} \cdot \frac{d}{d x}(x+y)$

or $\quad 3 x^2+3 y^2 \frac{d y}{d x}=\cos (x+y) \cdot\left(1+\frac{d y}{d x}\right)$

or $\quad\left[3 y^2-\cos (x+y)\right] \frac{d y}{d x}=\cos (x+y)-3 x^2$

$\therefore \quad \frac{\mathrm{dy}}{\mathrm{dx}}=\frac{\cos (\mathrm{x}+\mathrm{y})-3 \mathrm{x}^2}{3 \mathrm{y}^2-\cos (\mathrm{x}+\mathrm{y})}$
